home.feature1Body: Music for everyone. Most of our concerts are free to encourage discovery.
home.feature2Body: Discover the Saint-Maurice and Saint-Étienne churches in a new light, carried by the sound of the organ.
home.feature3Body: A moment of serenity and raw emotion. The king of instruments in all its majesty.
home.heritageBody: Beyond concerts, it is a millennial dialogue between architecture and music that we offer you. Each note resonates through Lille's history, offering a timeless parenthesis at the heart of the city.

about.title: Orgue Vivant
about.intro: Orgue Vivant is a community initiative to keep organ music alive in downtown Lille. We organise free or low-cost concerts, open to everyone.
about.stMaurice: The grand organ of Saint-Maurice was built in 1877 by the Maison Delmotte of Tournai, housed in a carved oak case crafted in 1878 by Lille cabinetmaker Charles Buisine-Rigot. The instrument underwent a major reconstruction in 1961 by Jean Pascal, who refounded approximately 85% of the organ while preserving the original case. With 48 stops across three manuals and pedal, four Barker machines, and a window console with 12 programmable combinations, it offers an exceptionally rich tonal palette. Its pitch is set at 435 Hz in equal temperament.
about.stEtienne: The gallery organ of Saint-Étienne is one of the most precious instruments in northern France. Built in 1840 by Daublaine-Callinet — a partnership between Louis Callinet and André Marie Daublaine — it was profoundly reworked in 1886 by master builder Aristide Cavaillé-Coll, then again in 1901 by Charles Mutin. Listed as a historical monument since 1980, this 42-stop instrument across three manuals and pedal bears witness to the evolution of French Romantic organ building. With its Barker machine on the Grand-Orgue and a pitch of 440 Hz, it remains a remarkably expressive instrument.
about.mission: Make organ music accessible, support the next generation of organists, and showcase Lille's musical heritage.
